Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

(The image? I've finished the sequence of my published books; while I decide what I want to do next, I have some memes to share.)

Ko-Fi

If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: habits, affirmations and gut feelings
comments.content:
Apologies this is a long one but hopefully of use to someone.
An intention for a while has been to change a couple of habits to see if doing that changes perceptual clarity regarding what seems to be happening with our society here in the UK. It’s something I’ve done at various times over the years as a sort of sanity check… a bit like fasting.

Anyway, sitting late last night and considering this, a thought came unexpectedly – “you need to do this now or fail”. Not so much a voice speaking as an announcement from somewhere and nowhere.
Kind of a surprise as in Whoa! Do what?
Since I had just been thinking about stopping drinking alcohol and smoking to check mental clarity – concluded it was about that – stopping not because I think those things are bad but in order to check perspectives. The crazy of the world just now seems like a good time to explore this. The Italians say: ‘A meal without wine is like a day without sunshine’, but I’ve been messing with that to give: ‘A mind without clarity is like a life without light’

Regardless, it was surprising and unexpected in its forcefulness and seeming seriousness.
Even though I do not know exactly what is meant, it was intense enough to properly consider the matter. My main area of interest is in esoteric arts, and concluded that the threat of failure could relate to that. Given where UK society looks to be heading, my inner practices have become more and more important as an anchor in an increasingly dysfunctional and chaotic whirlpool of a world, so failure through lack of effort would be galling!
The UK seems more like a tinder box masquerading as a house of cards all the time.

Failure in this is not an attractive option and to quote some fella called Yoda “Do or do not do; there is no try.”
And so I looked at my glass of red wine that was still 1/2 full and the rolling tobacco I was about to pick up and stopped; some consideration, and the wine went down the sink. Got some water, the tobacco pouch got put in its box and I sat wondering how this would play out.
The answer in mind was: ‘It’s up to you”

A day later and I have noticed certain triggers that might usually prompt a smoke; after catching that feeling , a surprisingly effective counter-measure has been to use a sort of affirmation set:

1. “A mind without clarity is like a life without light”
2. “I choose freedom of choice”
3. “I choose clarity through stillness”
Where:
1. is the interrupt to disrupt the trigger response – a thought stopper if you will
2. defines the choice as mine to make – not an auto response
3. not just words but a practical action that leads to a different destination to that of the trigger response

And am replacing the activity of smoking with other activities. Slightly tricky as I find that nicotine is good for focussed thought... so still experimenting with that.
I’ve done this sort of thing before and recall it was around a couple of weeks before new patterns seemed to establish and two to three months before a state of equilibrium was reached.
Like Eartha Kitt said: "I have things but things don't have me."

Anyway, maybe it’s something or maybe it’s nothing, but have been feeling a sort of build-up going on. Not particularly new as over the years things ebb and flow all the time. Read back 2000 years and it just looks like different tunes but very similar dances, and for the last 30 years I have been amazed at my ability to still be amazed at the world; but, has anyone else had any particularly strong 'gut feelings' recently?

EW

When I was a kid I was very respectful (and fearful) of authority as well as quiet, shy, and introverted. Around 10 or so I ended up at a church service with a friend (my family was not religious or read atheist materialist). I don't recall what denomination it was but it was one of the more fundamentalist protestant churches. The pastor was giving his sermon which eventually ended up focusing on me and my need to be "saved" and then he ended up aggressively demanding of me if I would "be saved" repeatedly in front of the whole congregation, which was probably 100 people. I was extremely frightened and upset. As he asked me the first time and everyone put their focus (and their prayers, I'm sure) towards me, I was absolutely quaking in my skin but a voice in my head that was not mine through all the panic very clearly and firmly told me, "NO". Each time he asked the voice repeated "NO" and I was able to squeak out a meek "no".

That was certainly a clear (if rather dated) example of this for me.

In much more recent examples, I had a... vision I suppose I'll call it... where a goddess took me to another world and showed me a huge gathering army and told me it was forming to bring ragnarok. At the end of the experience I had the presence of mind to ask her who she was and she told me she was Aphrodite. I asker her why she showed me that and she told me, to paraphrase, because the gods wanted me to see it.

I also had another experience around the same time of conversation with Odin, who told me to "get ready" though he didn't specify for what.

A few weeks ago I had a conversation with Idunn (who is a regular part of my practices) and she brought up both of these experiences and reiterated the "get ready". She also went on quite an exasperated tirade about how, again to paraphrase, absolutely retarded your society is at every level. (your in the sense of she was talking to me and drawing a clear line that she was not part of it).

As for the covid vax thing, my refusal came from a history of abuse by the medical system, a background in some of the actual science the "Science" was based off of and a deep seated don't tell me what to do cantankerousness rather than any great spiritual insight.

HV

https://equilibriabook.com/molochs-toolbox/

Most of it is a dialogue about the eight or so layers of recursion of what went wrong in the example of a regulatory judgement with force of law preventing premature infants from getting blatantly essential lipids, often causing obviously-to-be-expected liver failure and death or brain damage.

> VISITOR: What about everyone on your entire planet who could possibly care about babies dying? [...] So your medical specialists are borked [this is a techie slang for "broken", connoting a colorful and correction-resistant kind of broken]. From the magic-tower analogy, I assume your systems of learning are borked, and that means most of the parents whose responsibility it is to protect the child are borked. Your politicians are borked. Your voters are borked. Your planet has no Serious People who could be trusted to try alternative shoe designs, let alone lead the way on any more complex coordination problem. Your prediction markets, I suppose, are somehow borked [...] you don’t have any way to test things on a smaller scale? Your economists must somehow be borked [...] and your financial system is borked so that nobody can make a profit on saving those babies or doing anything else useful. I’m not stupid. I’ve picked up on the pattern at this point.

Each layer is made of people acting within the constraints of the incentives created by the people in the next layer up, and somehow the whole system goes off the rails and resists correction at all levels.

... Is there a name for the whole line of solutions to the problem of "who watches the watchmen" or "what will serve as the unincentivized incentivizer", that involve having a strong cultural symbolic economy, which makes a symbolic category of action equally the right/fitting/admirable thing to do, say, when you're in the position of highest power as when you're in some position of lesser power, or as when you're in some completely other ritual-created context in some symbolically corresponding situation?

I have short anecdote about voices, the Path, and also disregarding voices…

At one time I became a follower of Śiva — I still am — and carried a mala (beads) with me everywhere, saying his mantra.
I was constantly at odds with society, my parents and family; not bowing to or giving in to “conventions” or “the dark side”.
— One night I was up late wandering the streets of Seattle. Homeless, aimless with the sole exception of achieving final moksha and union with Śiva.
I wandered onto a sidewalk near a busy highway, and had this feeling, “I should go onto the highway, and walk along the middle of the road.”
I stood a minute, knowing that in the dark I would be very well hit and killed or maimed.

I threw my mala into the bushes — not wanting to achieve union that way. And I made a commitment to temporarily follow Yahweh, find a social worker in a homeless shelter, and eventually find an apartment to live in.

I have found my apartment, and practice internal alchemy here on my couch daily.

Moksha and Union are still a possibility.

a) Last week, there was a discussion of Scott Adams, affirmations, and cancer; someone noted that it made sense for certain affirmations being mishandled as a way to get free stuff from the universe to be a cause of cancer. I think it might be more general: cancer seems to be an occupational hazard of the mage. Some of this likely has clear environmental causes (ex: it would not surprise me if it turns out that certain forms of incense increase the risk of lung cancer), but it also makes an uncomfortable amount of sense in another way: every time anyone uses magic to fight the currents of the universe (which a lot of magic involves, given how hard it is for human beings to unify our wills with divine will), we are establishing a pattern of rejecting order and seeking what benefits us instead of accepting the needs and desires of the universe as a whole.

This pattern would also affect each of our cells, and this could easily explain why so many occultists seem to have died of the disease. Even in cases where the outcome is good from a human perspective, if the magic fights against what the universe would do otherwise, it would establish the pattern of seeking what is best for a subset of the cosmos; in the microcosm, that would be cells rejecting the needs of the body....

b) I'd like to add one point to the topic of addictions as discussed last week: the need being met is not always strictly emotional. A few years ago I broke a long standing addiction to fast food by adding sufficient salt to my food, because what was happening was my body was craving salt and getting it from the most convenient source. Once I decided to ignore the health warnings about too much salt, and just start using it, all interest in fast food vanished pretty much instantly.

c) I'd like to make sure I understand the principles behind karma. Rather than using abstractions, I'd like to use a concrete example: I've gone to the other side of the country to visit my grandma each year for most of my life; when I go visit I usually help her with a bunch of things, ranging from cooking for her to fixing furniture and replacing damaged household appliances. I usually fly despite the environmental impact, given that the state of inter-city transit in Canada is atrocious, but I do check to see if it would be viable to take trains/buses. Sometimes I get lucky, but usually it is not possible for me to take such a trip.

So from this, I become the kind of person who is more flexible about certain moral judgments (good and bad depending on the context); who spends time and effort to help elderly relatives (good); who is willing to engage in environmentally damaging activities (bad); who puts an effort into checking if other options are available before committing to a course of action (good); and who considers the trade offs in actions (good); who is willing to accept the humiliation and discomforts of air travel (bad), when in the pursuit of a clearly defined goal (good).

I vaguely remembered a Surgeon General's report from 1964 about smoking and health problems, so I did a quick search and found more information about it (1). This lead me to a document from the government of Canada that indicates our first official warning about tobacco came in 1954, almost immediately after the first "serious" studies linking smoking to lung cancer were published in 1950 (2).

This seems suspiciously close in timing to when this article (3) suggests that salt was linked to hypertension in a way the medical system gravitated toward, in 1948. Another article I found talks about how until the late 1940s, the general consensus among medical researchers was that salt had a minimal impact on hypertension, low salt diets might actually harm kidney function, and that efforts to replicate some of the key studies that suggest low salt might be be healthy failed (4). I'm having a hard time finding when the first health warnings began (and have spent enough time already), but it looks like they started sometime in the 1950s, because a book on daily life in the 1960s I have talks about how controversy when the American Heart Associate said everyone should cut back on salt in 1961, extending the warnings from merely those at risk for heart disease.

So it looks like sometime around 1948-1954 something changed and suddenly both salt and tobacco became public health enemies. In both cases, there seems to have been resistance by other parts of society (it seems to have been more marked with tobacco), but the medical system itself seems to have went to war against both at pretty close to the same time.

comments.title: Misadventures with ghost cats
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I have an update, of sorts, on the "ghost cat" situation I asked about last week.

It didn't appear again, though of course it's only been a week. I had to get rid of the vinegar dish after two days—turns out that acetic acid fumes are quite good at driving me away, too. The smell was alright but my eyes started hurting. At any rate, it seems like the sage had a much bigger effect. During Tuesday's GSF morning exercise, I could properly feel (what I assume to be) the One Life's flow through me whereas before, I'd started wondering whether I was accomplishing anything other than treating my neighbours to a vision of me standing in front of my bedroom window, staring outside in my pyjamas. Sadly, this has since faded quite a bit, though it's still better than before. I also felt a lot more refreshed waking up that morning than I have in a good few years.

Christophe's comment that the entity might not be malevolent but simply trying to get my attention, a sentiment echoed by comments on a different forum, intrigued me, so I consulted the SGO, something that I probably should've done earlier. This was my first non-daily divination (I'm new to all of this), so I second guessed myself a lot, but I settled on myself, the entity, my bedroom, and the entity's intention as the four factors I asked about. In order, I got: Cube reversed, Square & Diagonal reversed, Tetrahedron reversed, and Progression of Roots. My take on this is that I've fallen into a rut (definitely true) and that the entity arrived as a consequence of that, to help me change. The room's role in this seems to be just a general store of negative, disruptive energy, as evidenced by how well the cleansing worked.

Further comments on that other forum suggested I might try summoning the entity to get it to explain itself and I decided to call it into a scrying mirror. I tried to prepare the space with the Opening and Closing of the SoP (I haven't gotten all that far with the GSF work so far, though I'm trying to keep at it) but really struggled with visualising the cleansed sphere. I probably should've aborted the experiment right then but went ahead anyways. I can share a detailed report if you or anybody else thinks I should but, in the interest of brevity, here's a summary:

I saw quite a few images in the mirror but they kept switching so quickly I could make very few out. There were several animals, other beings, and objects without any (apparent) rhyme or reason. The mirror seemed to expand and start to take over my entire field of vision but whenever it got close, I had to blink which reset it to a normal mirror shape. This was frustrating but, at the same time, I occasionally had the idea that I could endanger myself this way. The mirror itself very occasionally looked like a cat or, at other times, a bug.

Verbally, I was stuck at asking the entity to show itself and telling it that I couldn't make out whatever it was trying to tell me. Mentally, a few questions directed at it popped into my head and I'm not entirely certain they originated with me. The answers were very confusing and contradictory. The entity identified itself as several species of animal as well as archangels Uriel and Michael. I don't think either of these claims were truthful but have no way of being sure.

I twice heard a meow and once steps whose location I couldn't quite make out.

I had the impression that I could've seen more if the mirror had been bigger or if I could just let it grow big enough.

After about subjective twenty to thirty minutes, I realised I wouldn't get any further and, on a very reckless whim, blew out the candle I was using for lighting in the hopes of seeing a little more in a dark room. The steps came back, this time probably in the hallway, and I got rather scared, fearing for my life. A license to depart and run to the lightswitch later, I felt reasonably safe again. I then discovered that the endeavour had actually taken me about an hour and that there were some blood (?) stains on my couch and one of its cushions. While I had started out kneeling on the cushion, I'd quickly discarded it and didn't have any open wounds on my legs anyways, though admittedly a few old ones. I had been nowhere near the couch.

In the following night, I had a bit of a nightmare: I decided to summon the entity again to find out more. This led to a lot of fire (possibly hellfire, though I can't say my vision of the afterlife ordinarily includes fire and brimstone).

A couple of hypotheses:

A) I was really dealing with the being I'd tried to call up. Given that I explictly asked for it to show itself, this seems like what should've happened. But as the divination and its own statements confirmed that it's not malicious, I really don't understand the outcome. Possibly some form of tough love? Or maybe it really is just very ambivalent.

B) I was dealing with a different entity. Perhaps I've driven the entity out by cleansing the room and something else noticed an opportunity to fuck with me. I'm unsure whether it was properly malicious as I don't think I was actually harmed.

C) I was dealing with several entities, possibly including the one I was trying to talk to. That would explain the quickly shifting images and the contradictory responses. It also explains why the footsteps seemed to come from outside the room while the mirror images obviously were inside.

My questions are these:

1) Which of the three hypotheses, if any, seems the most likely?

2) Did I make any obvious mistakes? I can supply a more detailed report too if that'd be helpful.

3) I'd still like to know more about this entity. Do you think it wise to pursue this?

4) Scary dreams notwithstanding, I'm not too concerned about trying the mirror again, provided I know what to do differently. Would you recommend other methods of inquiry instead or in addition to that?

5) I'm struggling with the SoP. Specifically, I can't make the sphere "grow", I can just picture successively bigger spheres. Is that a problem?

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Prayer, Queens and Druids
replies.content:
1) I should probably put that into the FAQ page sometime! Here's how it goes.

First, when you first wake up, before doing anything else besides using the bathroom, read one chapter of the Bible every day. (Rules for choosing the chapter are below.) Second, think about what you just read. Third, pray about it, ending with the Lord's Prayer. That's it. Do this every single day, first thing in the morning

As for choosing chapters, don't start with Genesis -- even if you get through the "Begats," as the woman in question used to say, your good intentions will get lost in the Wilderness with the Israelites somewhere in the second half of Exodus and never be seen again. Start with the Gospel of John. When you've finished it, go on to Matthew, Mark, and Luke, in that order; then Acts, the epistles, and Revelation, followed by the Psalms. Repeat this three times (this will take you several years) until you have the habit of daily Bible reading well established. Then read Genesis and the first half of Exodus, then jump to the last chapter of Deuteronomy and go through the rest of the Old Testament, then the New Testament. Repeat this three times. Then you'll have gotten good enough at extracting insight from Bible texts that you can tackle the three and a half books -- 2nd half of Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy -- that are mostly laws.

2) The organization to which she was admitted is the ritual side of the Welsh National Eisteddfod (Eisteddfod Genedlaethol Cymru), which you can read about here. The short form is that it's a competition for Welsh poets dating to the Middle Ages, but enriched in the early 19th century with Druid Revival trappings to provide local color. It's very establishment; other initiates include past Archbishop of Canterbury Rowan Williams.

3) Tsk, tsk -- if you read the article more closely you'll find that they're talking about American Druids, not British ones. As usual for the Grauniad, they're wrong -- the first Druid organization in the US, the Society of Ancient Druids, was founded in 1798 -- but the Reformed Druids of North America (RDNA), the order they talk about, does exist; in fact, I'm a Third Order initiate of it.

comments.title: Mental state during ritual
comments.content:
Good morning, JMG. I hope you're doing well.

I had a question concerning mental states and ceremonial magic.

I've this notion that my headspace during ritual should be nominally different than my mundane day-to-day headspace. As such, during ritual I try to keep my mind uncluttered, focused, and tranquil, reminding myself to act (or to try to act) as an unobstructed channel for the descent of higher powers. However, beyond this I am unsure what state to cultivate in my mind.

Should a budding magician try to cultivate a hyper-lucid, energized, self-aware state during ritual? Or a more drowsy, 'alpha-brainwave', liminal headspace so that I can lose myself in ritual and let my subconscious do the work? Or something entirely different perhaps?

Apologies if the question doesn't make sense. I've been experimenting with different approaches and keeping notes in my journal; so far I haven't noticed a personal preference and I haven't yet gained the inner perception needed to know how these states affect the working of the rituals.

As always, thank you for your time and for hosting MM.

comments.content:
Happy Monday,

JMG - in some podcasts you’ve mentioned that ceremonial magic is a powerful path that can work very well - or go horribly wrong…

As a practitioner of CM, I’d like to think the former applies to me. I was hoping you could elaborate a bit:

1) what does CM going horribly wrong look like?

2) any tips to avoid some of the common (or less common) pitfalls?



Thank you !

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Ceremonial magic is like a race car with a powerful engine -- the number of ways it can go wrong is limited only by the number of objects along the road the driver can slam into at high speed. Still, there are two broad categories of failure, one caused by too much ego, the other by too little.

a) The ego-excessive form happens when the practitioner is motivated by pride and tries to go too far too fast, and attempts to achieve the consciousness of the Higher Self without getting his ego housebroken first. What happens in this case is that the ego gets projected and then overinflated to a spectacular degree. Uncontrolled rage fits in the face of disagreement and corrupt, exploitive behavior directed at followers and students are common side effects. This is what Zen masters have in mind when they say that someone who gains sudden, complete enlightenment is like an arrow shot straight into Hell.

b) The ego-deficient form form happens when the practitioner is motivated by greed and becomes dependent on spirits for magical benefits. What happens in this case is that the ego breaks up into fragmentary personae; the practitioner's state of consciousness gradually sinks to the level of the spirits they summon; their minds and personalities become rigid and oddly mechanical, their ethics (if they ever had any) go gurgling down the drain, and they typically end up being drawn into substance abuse, sadomasochistic excesses, and any other debasement the spirits toying with them happen to favor. These are the spiritual leaders who get busted for molesting children or petty crime or what have you.

2) That's easy enough. The first requirement is to make daily meditation an invariable part of your spiritual practices, and not the kind that silences the mind, either. You need your mind clear and active to follow the Path effectively; this is why I recommend discursive meditation as an essential part of occult training. The second requirement is to make the conjuration of spirits an occasional practice at most, and to avoid evil spirits -- angel magic and work with intelligences is much more productive. The third, and most difficult for most people nowadays, is to make genuine prayer part of your life. Notice that I don't specify which deity or deities to invoke, and I'm not expecting or encouraging you to get into the groveling "Oh, I'm such a miserable lump of snot before Thee" sort of spiritual masochism that's fashionable in some faiths and some circles. Approach the Divine as you would approach a much older and wiser friend; talk; and then listen.

replies.content:
There is a book that's somewhat along the lines of what you mention, Luke, about the Christianization of Europe--it's not exactly what you're looking for but one can infer quite a bit from it. It's called "The Germanization of Early Medieval Christianity: A Sociohistorical Approach to Religious Transformation" by James C. Russell.

This is a sociology book that looks at the conditions that cause a culture to be open to religious conversion, versus cultures which are not open to that and resist it. It uses the medieval Germanic cultures as its focus, which were a real challenge to the efforts of the Christian missionaries. The author spends about half the book studying--very insightfully--what it was about Germanic paganism that made them uninterested and unwilling to convert to Christianity, and in some places takes it all the way back to Indo-European culture.

The author's conclusion was that conversion of the Germanic Heathens required so much spin-doctoring, adjustment, adaptation, and obfuscation of Christian principles and practices to convince the Germanic peoples to convert, that the Christianity which came out the other end of the process was as much 'Germanized' as the Germanic peoples were Christianized--an outcome which changed Christianity almost as much as it changed Germanic paganism. Hence the title of his book. It's a really fascinating study; as a Heathen scholar I learned a lot from it myself.

In my own biased opinion, this result was not to the betterment of either religion, really, which provokes a lot of thought along the lines of what you're looking for. After studying this book I think that both Heathenry (obviously) but also Christianity would have been better off going their separate ways. For those interested in Christian history, there are also interesting insights that can be drawn regarding the differences between Western (Germanic and Roman influenced) Christianity versus Eastern Orthodox forms of Christianity as well as the often very different forms of early Christianity that developed in Middle Eastern regions.

It sounds like you're more interested in Roman paganism, but there's a great deal to be learned from the book about Indo-European pagan thought and practice as a whole, as contrasted to Christianity.

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: Desire
comments.content:
In the Cosmic Doctrine, Dion fortune says that the path beyond a material existence requires the elimination of desire, then fear, then a death/resurrection.

It's easy to see how if you don't desire anything, fear falls away. But to truly eliminate desire would involve eliminating even basic survival desires like to eat or drink or move anywhere at all. Maybe such a commitment would indeed lead one to finish one's incarnation rather quickly, but as I see it, eliminating desire in totality isn't practical.

Even if we do eliminate it, presumably we have to take some kind of action in the next stage of living, which is driven by desire. I guess my question is: does the elimination of desire refer to each subplane? For example, I can eliminate certain low desires, those that are blaring in my face and distracting me, so that desires on higher planes can have their say. If I stop desiring heroin and fame, for example, I can hear the desire to be a loving person involved in loving relationships. It seems more a matter of attuning to higher desires (which would in turn produce higher-level fears that those desires aren't met).

Even the Solar Logos must have desires on the spiritual plane, which are effectively choices to do one thing as opposed to another.

Is that what she is getting at? The problem with my subplane theory is that it mostly deals with the astral and etheric desires rising up, because we still can't eliminate the physical instinctual desire to breathe, drink, etc. without leaving the material plane, which is providing us with essential learning experiences. Maybe a desire doesn't exist at a physical level, then, and it only comes in when we feel certain discomforts at the physical level?

replies.title: Re: Desire
replies.content: That was a common notion in Theosophy, borrowed from Western misunderstandings of Buddhism. It's a bad idea. As Plato pointed out, desire needs to be educated, not eradicated; all our desires, he argued, are ultimately part of the force that leads us toward the knowledge of the One, and as we recognize this, harmful desires dissolve and helpful desires are born.

I’ve recently started planting and tending a garden of herbs and vegetables. I’ve planted two apple trees, one of which is thriving and the other failing, despite giving them identical care/water/sunshine.

I was wondering which deities from the Druidical tree of life pantheon would be best to pray to for blessing plants and garden?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Gardening Gods?
replies.content: In the Druid Revival traditions which I studied, Hu the Mighty governs rain and good weather conditions, Ced the Earth-Mother (or Ceridwen, who is an aspect of Ced) governs soil fertility, and the maiden goddess Niwalen rules plant growth. You can invoke all of them. I wonder if a little soil supplementation around the failing apple tree would help -- that sounds like it got planted in a patch of depleted soil.

replies.title: Re: Gardening Gods?
replies.content: Is Hesus, chief of the tree spirits, also part of that particular pantheon or is he a part of some other tradition?

replies.title: Re: Gardening Gods?
replies.content: He's part of the same system, but you'd invoke him to use the tree or some part of it for medicine, magic, or divination.

replies.title: Re: Gardening Gods?
replies.content: Very helpful - thank you!! I was thinking Ced and Olwen too - I didn’t know about Hu and the weather.  I’ll add some compost to the soil and see if that helps also. This summer has been so hot, it’s hard to gauge how much these young trees need for watering.

comments.title: Questions on Aphorisms on Astrology and Magic
comments.content:
I'm using the Aphorisms as my current series of themes for discursive meditation and have some questions that have come out of my meditations. While I haven't read any of the books you suggest in the Notes for Practitioners, I have re-read your essay on understanding Renaissance magic in A Magical Education, to give me a bit of background in the world-view of the text. I think working with the Aphorisms will aid in developing my mental body, and it might help me to better make use of natural magic, since it seems to be based on the model of Renaissance magic.

Since the concepts of species and rays are so central to the first dozen Aphorisms (that's as far as I have gotten), I would like to ask some questions about them.

1. Does the concept of the species have Neoplatonic roots? It seems to me that species exist on the noetic level of Neoplatonism based on your essay about it in Ascendant, but my background in philosophy is too thin and patchy to know if I understand correctly.

2. If so, I thought that Linnaeus may have borrowed the term from Neoplatonism for use in defining a biological species. Is that the case?

3. If I understand correctly, each of us humans radiates out a ray which is recognizable as the ray of the human species, but for each of us, that ray is modified to a degree according to the particular accidents that distinguish us from other humans. The mage uses knowledge of how the accidents modify the ray I give out to do magic that is tailored to make a desired change in me. For instance, if I am feeling ill, the accidents of my humoral composition and how the illness affects it could be matched to herbs whose rays would be in sympathy with my base state of humoral health and/or would be in antipathy with the changes the illness had caused. Am I on the right track?

This is very different from the Lévi theory of magic - which in turn suggests that there are many different theories of magic across the different human cultures of the past, present, and future. Each does some things well and cannot do others, because each of them reduces the complexity of the cosmos into a model, and models by their nature have to leave something out.

replies.title: Re: Questions on Aphorisms on Astrology and Magic
replies.content:
Delighted to hear this!

1) The distinction between species and genus was common to all the ancient philosophers. Yes, species are the simplest things you find on the noetic level.

2) He lifted it from medieval philosophy, where it was a constant theme of discussion.

3) Yes, exactly -- and yes, there are many different theories of magic. The world is too complex and too subtle for human language and human thought!

comments.title: Proof it's Real
comments.content:
Mr. Greer,



You sometimes refer to an “oh shit” moment where the beginning practitioner gets “proof” that magic is real, that there are in fact unseen forces and consciousnesses that we are interacting with.

As someone who was raised by dogmatic materialist atheists, it can be really hard for me to get a felt sense of these other consciousnesses. The part of me who explains things away as coincidences and pattern seeking is so loud and pees all over the “meaningful coincidences”. I feel like whatever sense I get turns to sand and slips through my fingers. Is there anything I can do to either elicit an experience that this “rational” mind cannot so easily dismiss? Or do I need to adjust my perspective? I feel like this lack of a felt sense of connection to something greater or beyond is at the root of anytime I go into a downward spiral of depression, existential angst, suicidality. I think of how even 12-step programs emphasize that they don’t work without the faith in a higher power. I want to develop my relationship with these other forms of consciousness for myself, without having to rely on information put out by others (gnosis, I suppose).

Thank you, for everything you share.

replies.title: Re: Proof it's Real
replies.content: The rational or, rather, rationalizing mind can dismiss anything. No matter what experience you have, the pseudorational filter imposed by modern thought can redefine it as something that fits inside the stifling limits of the materialist worldview. My advice is to try not to worry about that, and pursue a set of practices as if you believed in them. You'll find that over time, the filter breaks down and you'll discover that you've started noticing just how much magic is in the world all along.

replies.title: Re: Proof it's Real
replies.content:
Yes, you can spend as much time as possible in natural environments where human impact is minimal, such as forests, deserts, seashores, prairies, stream banks, etc. Of course, that means bringing along as little man-made distraction with you as well (which may well include other distracting humans!) The temptation to listen to wonderful music or mystical teachings while walking about may seem like efficient multi-tasking, which it may well be, but it will automatically draw some of your focus away from your willed intention of drawing out experiences that your conscious mind cannot easily overwrite.

Direct your focus to observing, hearing, smelling, and touching the natural environment you're in. Notice your thoughts and body experiences as a part of that natural environment. Notice the repetitive thoughts that you habitually implement to distract your attention away from being present in natural environments. Once those gatekeeping thoughts become familiar, let them act as red flags to help you return your awareness to the natural environment all around and within you.

Rationalism is not a natural state for us to dwell in; it requires a fairly constant stream of reinforcing mental chatter to keep awareness of all the non-rational at bay. Oddly, much of that trained and rehearsed mental chatter is not itself rational in any way, but, let's face it, neither is rationalism when you get down to it. The obsessive competing, comparing, calculating, ranking, and categorizing that our schooling so over-emphasizes is but one tiny part of all the amazing feats our monkey brains can accomplish... and not the most enchanting part by far!

Our monkey brains can detect subtle defects in the wonky models we use to make sense of the reality we find ourselves in. Our monkey brains can catch fleeting glimpses of the deep wisdom of our ancient souls. Our monkey brains can come to recognize when greater patterns from divine realms are influencing our material plane. Our monkey brains can wonder in awe at the vast forces guiding us through this wonderful cosmos, so much greater than our tiny comprehension. Our monkey brains can name those divine patterns Venus and Jupiter or Gravity and Space or any other set of divine labels we choose to focus on. And our monkey brains can pretend that we have not already developed a relationship with these other forms of consciousness, although the gods know that is just a moody pretense on our part.

Let the sand slip through your fingers, and listen to the sound of its falling. Smell the wind, the same wind by which animals and plants are smelling you. Feel the bugs crawling about on your skin, as you are on the earth's. Notice the forest; notice the trees; notice them at the same time; then notice the sense of connection to something greater than any individual tree. There are in fact unseen forces and consciousnesses that we are interacting with at all times... beginning, quite naturally, with our own.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Proof it's Real
replies.content:
If you pay attention to what happens when people try to produce proofs that would be sufficiently un-interpret-awayable for sufficiently large masses of other people -- such as public limb regenerations, or parapsychology experiments replicating in an unambiguously not-file-drawer-effect not-fraud way -- only certain hypotheses about supernatural cosmology survive. Those surviving hypotheses more or less amount in practice to: "you will only ever get to have the sort of evidence such that, if you end up having willfully or culpably falsified your judgement about it, only you or maybe the people closest to you will ever be in a position to be reasonably sure whether or not you had done so".

Infelicitously, this means that most supernatural senses/experiences for most humans most of the time will be locked behind the sorts of programs of training that will allow skeptics not closely familiar with you and your circumstances to credibly say "oh, they were just cultivating pareidolias and propensities to confabulate 'supernatural' impressions, making themselves suggestible according to cultural paradigms for the supernatural, look at those randomly confabulated sensations that were confidently reported by those reiki nurses in that experiment with the kid with her hand in the wooden box", or locked behind the kinds of strange cognitive events that will allow skeptics not closely familiar with you and your circumstances to credibly say "oh, they were just having a temporal lobe seizure / misattributed memory / strategically-acting subpersonality which had finally accumulated enough motivational capital to make one decisive guerrilla psychological strike / [what-have-you]". Because, if those experiences weren't locked that way, then somehow too many people would no longer be in a position of being able to interpret them away, which apparently somehow matters to something having the relevant power to enforce its preferred outcomes, for some unknown reason.

One possible takeaway is that you should only be pursuing such practices if you trust your judgement in matters of interpretation that are at least that subtle, or rather that you trust your judgement more than you trust alternative sources of judgement. I want to say that understanding Bayesian reasoning helps a lot with knowing how to safely go from black-and-white reasoning of "reliable"/"not reliable" to intersecting combinatorial shades of plausibility. But because the range of possible explanatory entities and phenomena (and compositions thereof) is so wide, probably it doesn't actually help all that much unless you can start getting into the kinds of applied-math judgements and formulations they use in statistical field theory.

Another possible takeaway is that, for the most part, the lessons of materiality are the important thing right now, and, circumstantially speaking, the supernatural is only important in such a way as to justify decisions that are still within a relatively close range of deviation from the habits that would have been appropriate to a purely material universe (with the size of that range perhaps being different for different people).

comments.title: help with local nature spirits
comments.content:
We seem to be having an issue with the local nature spirits.

The background: For the first 4 years that we lived in this area I was doing Druidic ritual work regularly, including the solstice/equinox workings of the Celtic Golden Dawn. In the last year I've switched up the structure of my practice in order to develop my own material.

From roughly the first time I did the equinox ritual here until recently, we had a very positive relationship with nature generally-- indicating a positive relationship with the spirits of the land. This includes: A regular sense of a beneficent presence in the land; the woods filled nearly to bursting with berries and with medicinal herbs; very pleasant weather for this climate; no problems with poisonous plants or insects.

This year, beginning in the Spring, things have been very different. Nearly all of the patches of useful herbs in the woods are gone, even the mulleins and the mints are gone. Such berries as appeared this year have been sporadic and shriveled; even the mulberry trees are empty. Poison ivy is everywhere. Our deck is infested with wasps, and as I write this my daughter is recovering from having been swarmed by them. That "sense of presence" is gone. The weather has been so horrific I've barely been outside all summer.

Divination initially suggested that my change in practice was the cause. I had thought of it as the local spirits having been offended. I haven't neglected them altogether, mind: I still pour out libations on a daily basis, for example. But I began to wonder if this unpleasant state of affairs might actually be the way things generally are here, and if my practice of the Druidic rituals helped to "raise the vibration" of the local landscape. Divination suggests this is in fact the case: A geomantic chart has Albus in the First House, Populus in the Eighth, Conjunctio in the Ninth, with perfection produced by Amissio in the Twelfth House and the Tenth (if you take it as a Ninth House question) and Seventh (if you take it as a First House question). Albus is also in the 3rd, and the index points to Laetitia in the Fourth.

Given this situation, do you have any advice on how to either repair my relationship with the local land spirits? I could develop a variation on the solstice/equinox ritual, summon some elementals or the elemental kings and ask, or something else-- Have you encountered a situation like this, and do you have any thoughts?

replies.title: Re: help with local nature spirits
replies.content: In your place I'd take a very hard look at the workings I started doing, and consider the possibility that they're not as suitable as you think. If I'd gotten that kind of effect when I was creating the Celtic Golden Dawn material, I'd have ditched the whole project on the spot.

replies.title: Re: help with local nature spirits
replies.content:
That's good advice. Hard advice, but good, which is why I asked you!

I meant to post this anonymously, and didn't realize I had signed into Dreamwidth. I know some people here know who I am, so I should probably add that the material I'm describing isn't anything that I've published or that's in any of the books I've been writing: I've had nothing but positive results from those. It was material for a future project, which I'll now have to seriously reconsider.

comments.title: Mirrors & Magic
comments.content:
I'm wondering about mirrors. There's definitely more to them than the immediate concept of a reflective surface. There are various cultural references to possible supernatural qualities:
Lewis Carroll's sequel "Through the Looking Glass" in which bizarro land is reached through a mirror, and other stories of traversing mirrors as portals; the old myth that if you break a mirror, you'll have bad luck for seven years; the legend of bloody mary; stories of people or ghosts not appearing in mirrors, or their reflection acting as an independent entity, and so on.
Anyway, can you sort out these various tales? What sort of magical qualities are associated with mirrors, and should we be careful around them?

replies.title: Re: Mirrors & Magic
replies.content: That's a fascinating question. Mirrors are among other things very good ways to access the astral plane -- scrying mirrors were once as popular as crystal balls -- and if you happen to be astrally sensitive, they can trigger unexpected visions. I'm not sure about the rest of it, though, and wonder what a thorough study of mirror folklore would turn up.

replies.title: Re: Mirrors & Magic
replies.content:
I see, thanks!

The astral connection would likely explain a fair amount of this, but I'm sure there's more to the story.

replies.title: Re: Mirrors & Magic
replies.content:
To add another bit of mirror folklore, the German Goddess or White Lady Walburga / Walpurga has been described as running or fleeing around the landscape, and her attributes have been described as well. Among them, she’s said to carry a three-cornered mirror. This is a very odd shape for a mirror.

My own interpretation is that the mirror is a reference to the Well of Wyrd, with the three corners representing spaces for the three Norns or Wyrd Sisters to sit around the mirror / Well. Granted, the Norns are Norse and Walburga is German, but the Fates show up in threes as “fairy godmothers” in German folklore as well. Walburga is known to help girls with their efforts at prognostication, such as divining who their future husband will be, among her other helpful deeds.

I read this description in a German study from the 1800s showing how traces of German Goddesses reappear in the attributes of Christian saints. Walpurga, in the tales about her, is identified as a saint, a Christian abbess. Yet no spirit of an abbess is going to appear in a white gown, golden crown and shoes, carrying the three-cornered mirror and dashing around the countryside having hair-raising adventures in the dark of night! Folklore is delightful!

Winifred

replies.title: Re: Mirrors & Magic
replies.content:
Interesting stuff, thanks.

I forgot to add another example -- in one of my favorite games, A Link to the Past, you use a mirror to teleport back to Light World from Dark World (which are two parallel worlds, one mostly a normal fantasy setting, the other corrupted and full of monsters).

replies.title: Re: Mirrors & Magic
replies.content: That reminds me of the way that Elias (the prophet Elijah in English translations) became a substitute for Helios in Christianized Greece, traveling across the sky with his fiery chariot!

replies.title: Re: Mirrors & Magic
replies.content:
As for why almost-material entities such as vampires and ghosts wouldn't show up in mirrors, there's a worthwhile relevant speculation about how apparitions work in the following comment, although it's in the part of the comment that's gated behind whether or not you expect to be safe from psychosis for the foreseeable future.

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/300859.html?thread=51822651

My conjecture is that etheric phenomena exist in a realm adjacent to a process for weighing possibilities for what kinds of experiences people could be caused to have, relatively close to that part of the process which corresponds to simple perceptual experiences, but still at some remove from locked-together proper physical consistency. (But note that physical consistency is only a very high priority, rather than being the only priority, so full consistency is not achieved. If it really were achieved (including statistical consistency), then we would be in an indistinguishably materialist universe, rather than just somewhere between "almost-almost-almost-indistinguishably" and "almost-indistinguishably".) If you're trying to set up a possibility like "the subject visually perceives thus-and-such phantasmal entity", and full quasi-physical consistency is a second priority, and you're mostly thinking first in terms of how you would be generating hypothetical contents for the subject's visual field, then your first priority is just going to be to put the perceived entity in the relevant part of the subject's visual field with vaguely the right details. It might take significant extra work to notice where there might be mirrors in the subject's visual field, that would be adding quasi-physical perceptual consistency constraints about realistic reflections on top of that.

(This is also something that happens with image-generating giant-trained-blobs-of-arithmetic-operations, although mostly they get the reflections wrong rather than just omitting them entirely.)

comments.title: Advice on how to explore Martinism
comments.content:
Hi John, I read with great interest your recent attendance at a Martinist event. I am seeking a mystic organization where I might fit in well and develop deep friendships. I am not much interested in "on line" forums, even less in "Zoom" virtual meetings. I want to find an organization I can physically attend, even if that involves a bit of periodic travel.

FWIW, I previously joined a vanilla Freemason lodge, only to be very disappointed that it didn't seem to have much of a spiritual, much less mystic, dimension. I also previously sampled a couple of magical orders, only to find I am, maybe, too much of a monotheist and, although I would appreciate well-intended, personal, mentoring, I don't want overwhelming structure and hierarchy. (Maybe too much Catholicism).

I guess I should also say I've been applying what I think you would call relatively-unstructured "magic", quite sucessfully, and naturally, for decades, toward changing myself and overcoming major life impediments. I just visualize and pray a lot to "life" and, with great focus, "impossible" things happen. (I've also had an experience, or two, when I applied this "wrongly" and reaped the whirlwind, so I try to be more careful these days).

When I search "Martinism" I get many hits and read a fair amount at various web-sites. Wikipedia (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martinism) says "Martinism as we know it today can be divided into three forms", although it seems like it is fractured than that when you layer on all the controlling organizations.

I would appreciate maybe two or three organizations you feel good about and any suggestions on how I might be able to talk to anyone, or find a local chapter or two where I might visit, to see if I might be a good fit.

Thank you for any advice or other suggestions you might have.

-gnat

replies.title: Re: Advice on how to explore Martinism
replies.content:
The Martinist scene these days is very fragmented, with more than a dozen independent bodies in the US alone (though there are starting to be some steps toward mutual recognition among certain bodies). Most of them admit members primarily by personal recommendation. I've only interacted with one Martinist order so far, and don't have a clear sense of the quality of the others; I do have some contacts, though, and if you drop me a message marked "not for posting" with your email address and your approximate location I'll see if I can help you find something. There's a chapter forming here in Rhode Island, for whatever that's worth. ;-)

As for vanilla Freemasonry -- I like that label! -- I think of it as the fossilized skeleton of an esoteric tradition, from which the life, the flesh, and the blood have all long since departed. (Masonosaurus Rex?) Its one great advantage to the occultist is that quite often, if you keep your ears open, you can locate the lodge or appendant body where all the occultists hang out, and make contacts that way.

replies.title: Re: Advice on how to explore Martinism
replies.content:
As an esoteric Freemason, let me just say that you should visit more Lodges. I got really lucky in finding a lot of esoterically minded brothers at my Lodge, but another Lodge that meets in the same building is the "vanilla' variety. It is almost physically painful to sit in on their ritual, for me. Had I joined that one, I likely would have been supremely disappointed too.

This also seems to be largely jurisdictional. I have heard that bringing up the word esoteric in Florida, for example, could find yourself facing masonic charges (I've only heard this from one disillusioned brother, but take his word for it). While in my home state of Arizona, we have a lot of strong esoteric lodges.

But even then, there are also a number of hidden, invite only, appendant bodies in every state (I know because I am part of one). Many of them want to see you doing good work in your Lodge though, before they are going to approach you.

If you ever find yourself in AZ, let me know, and I'll introduce you to a bunch of like minded brothers.

-Trubrujah

replies.title: Re: Advice on how to explore Martinism
replies.content: You were very lucky. I've belonged to close to a dozen lodges in my two and a half decades in the Craft and only one of those was even comfortable with esotericism. I didn't have too much trouble finding the hideouts where esoteric Masons hang out, but then I was already in print by then.

replies.title: Re: Advice on how to explore Martinism
replies.content:
There are still a bunch of old timers of the dinner club variety at my Lodge who roll their eyes when we start talking about esotericism, but a lot of the younger guys are hungry for it here in AZ. Many, its what got them interested in the first place. And we are slowly growing in number at my Lodge.

Now, when we get someone interested in Freemasonry who expresses an interest in the esoteric, even the old fork and plate masons will come and get one of us "esoterics" to chat with him, while before they might have brushed it off.

We are also lucky, in that our Grand Lecturer for the state Grand Lodge is a huge esoteric, so we are seeing more programs come out with an emphasis on it. It really feels like an esoteric Renaissance of sorts here in AZ Freemasonry.

-Trubrujah

replies.title: Re: Advice on how to explore Martinism

replies.title: Re: Cars
replies.content:
Machines move the air rather quickly which leads to the formation of vortices. Even small vortices (the size of a fist, for example) are surprisingly stable and move quickly - if one "hits" you, well it feels just like that. If you want to impress your neighbor, you can use this phenomenon to extinguish a candle by snapping your hand quickly close to the flame (or with a crisp karate punch, for that matter). That might be part of it - the air gives you a "beating". I can imagine that vortices which are created by more natural means usually have different proportions which makes it more comfortable to interact with them - but the latter is only a guess.

Small bonus for all, who like to like to tinker a little bit: Take bucket, cut a hole in the bottom - maybe 2 to 4 inches in diameter - and close the top of the bucket tightly with a membrane (for example cut out of a plastic bag) and adhesive tape. If you now punch on the membrane, the bucket will eject a small but fast vortex that travels in a more or less straight line. You can use it to "punch" somebody (don't worry, it won't hurt ;-) ), extinguish a candle at quite a distance (15 feet is possible, if you have a solid construction), etc. If you fill the bucket with smoke, you can see the vortices. Any kind of smoke will produce some effect, but if you have even access to a fog machine, this becomes gorgeous. Have fun!

Cheers,

replies.title: Re: Cars
replies.content:
Temple tech researcher here. Movement has a quality of its own, and the nature of that movement does generate ripples in the environment (mostly propagating out at something equivalent to Newton's inverse square law). The modern world loves quantity, understanding that air moving out from a machine, or a car, in a valueless, qualityless fashion. We can tell you volumetric measures, maybe even dust counts, but we can't tell you a thing about your experience in interacting with that air.

Incidentally, this is where the sense of smell (amongst other senses come in). Our brains are hardwired, through smell and taste to understand and detect the qualities of that air. Science doesn't yet have the foggiest idea of how we do that. This isn't a "magic of gaps" argument, some of the sense of smell will be entirely related to the optical properties of objects - when we transform molecules with mechanical systems (and a lack of specific intent), they come out without optical qualities at all (racemic mixtures in chemistry), for most intents and purposes, dead. If its been transformed or otherwise interacted with a living system, molecules come out optically polarised. Why - we don't have a clue.

And from there the rabbit hole goes far deeper beyond things we can detect with our instruments, to things that our consciousness is attuned to.

Think of the effect on a sensitive person of a natural perfume, versus an artificial one (even if the underlying molecules are the same).

replies.title: Re: Cars
replies.content:
The book you may want is Elixir, A Story of Perfume, Science and the Search for the Secret of Life by Theresa Levitt.

It's an outstanding summary of the transition basically from applied alchemy to modern chemistry, and in a way, a biography of the French chemists who basically created modern chemistry, and in doing so, just canceled whole concepts. There's a direct line from Auguste Laurent, who basically discovered enatomiers (and got completely shunned), Louis Pasteur, who learned it from Laurent, but was smart enough to stay completely silent about what he found (until very recently), and then direct through to Reich who repeated similar experiments, complete with the same cancelling.

It's the extreme nature of the cancelling of life force experiments that makes me think, they are onto an aspect of the temple tech.

Anyone who gets too close to it gets canceled, unless, of course, you've already been canceled and don't care ;)

replies.title: Re: Cars

I've had some thoughts on this lately, and have formulated a hypothesis about this. Nearly every form of artificial method of moving air uses metals and electricity. Electricity generates magnetism, and in the presence of magnetism, nearly all metals become at least slightly magnetic. Officially, the effects are too small to notice, but I'm not so sure: a lot of the sufferings from people with EMF look a lot like "electricity sickness" from the late 19th and early 20th century, and there are studies that showed that adding wires running through the walls of a classroom made the kids grow slightly heavier.

Thus, I think that the case can be made for human physiology being a lot more sensitive to electromagnetic effects; and that the effects this has on air might play a role in why some people (but not all; just like with EMF some people seem more sensitive to it than others) find air circulated by electric fans unpleasant.

replies.title: (no subject)

It made it much easier for me to fall asleep a lot faster, and it more or less shut down a lot of the usual issues with anxiety and the like. comments.title: Acts of restitution for past harms
comments.content:
It’s been several months since I established a ‘worth-ship full’ relationship with Åine, a minor deity in the Irish pantheon, associated with radiance and abundance, and set up a little shrine to her, where I express gratitude for the minor – yet to me very important – abundances that Åine has provided, which allow me to provide for my family in a time when things could easily have gone very badly.

[IMAGE:0]

In addition to expressing my gratitude directly, I’ve found that I have rather naturally cleaned up my spiritual act, and no longer can be found hanging out in the dive bars of the rougher quarters of the lower astral. (There are some attitudes Åine just won’t tolerate, and in any case she deserves to have a better class of follower than that!)

This all has led me to reflect back on and regret the sleazier and more opportunistic and thoughtless acts I have committed in what has mostly been a reasonably honourable life. Some of the harms I reflect on are to people, beings, and institutions that no longer exist, and I wonder what might be appropriate methods of restitution for these?

I’m not looking for a karmic get out of jail free card – I don’t think they issue those – but I do feel that some form of reparations and acknowledgement is correct and proper, in addition to accepting whatever karmic effects I reap for past actions with as much grace as I can muster.

Can you suggest some traditional ways to make spiritual restitution for past harmful acts? I hear about Planetary Charity for example, but am not sure if it would apply, or if I would need to know astrological information I lack, such as the time of my birth, to find the correct timing for such acts.

replies.title: Re: Acts of restitution for past harms
replies.content: This is very good to hear, and the shrine is classic -- simple, elegant, and appropriate. As for the karmic issues, I'd encourage you to pray to your goddess for guidance. Deities know about these things, and will send opportunities your way.

replies.title: Re: Acts of restitution for past harms
replies.content: Hi, what would you recommend to do if I want to establish a relationship with Åine?

replies.title: Re: Acts of restitution for past harms
replies.content:
For me it was a serendipitous thing. I’d been practicing the SoP for a year or so, which I think was helpful preparation to recognizing a deity when I encountered one. I was also going though a very stressful bureaucratic process that could go well or badly and influence our life for years to come based on seemingly random choices whose outcome was deeply uncertain. There was a point of intervention where a course of action became clear to me, and at that moment I also felt that Åine – a deity I knew only as a name on a list of sun- and fire-related gods – was directly guiding the choice.

It just fit after that, and I learned more about the deity, and went on from there. I never set out to create a relationship, and I expect that is more common than not. It’s kind of like falling in love – it’s not something that can be forced or directed, in my experience.

I also think that the element of meaningful and fateful 'chance' that I describe is likely a doorway to meeting a deity or other spiritual entity. I speculate that there is something about critical points of high-stakes random chance in life that opens a door to these encounters. (Our host may have some thoughts on that aspect).

I’m sure that for skilled mages there are ways to seek and connect with specific deities, but I feel that for myself, and perhaps many others, it is better to simply be open to what may or may not occur, and build the awareness needed to notice when it does.

replies.title: Re: Acts of restitution for past harms
replies.content:
What a beautiful shrine!! Thank you for sharing it with us.

As for restitution, years ago I developed a ceremony and procedures specifically for that purpose. It is Heathen oriented, but could easily be adapted to a mythical setting suitable for you. If you’d like to take a look at it, it’s posted on my website, HeathenSoulLore.net. The title is “Threads of Wyrd and Scyld: A Ninefold Rite of Life Renewal.” You can find it under the menu header Orlog, Wyrd, and Luck.

comments.title: AC, air humidity, and etheric energy
comments.content: I've long felt that air conditioned air feels dead; as if the energy has been drained out of it. I'm wondering if this is the case now, because the technology also dehumidifies the air. Air can only hold so much water vapour; how much depends on temperature. The coils that cool the air when air conditioning is running are set up to also dehumidify the air, and the water drains off the coils into a tank. It is a quite ingenious system, but it has some flaws. Do you think that this might be the reason the air feels dead: that this newly condensed water has drained the etheric energy of the air?

replies.title: Re: AC, air humidity, and etheric energy
replies.content: That may be part of it. I haven't noticed, though, that naturally dry air feels dead, the way air conditioned air does.

replies.title: Re: AC, air humidity, and etheric energy
replies.content:
Well, naturally dried air doesn't have a set of coils condensing water and cooling them to 40 degrees Fahrenheit (which I understand is the temperature where water holds the most etheric energy) and then disposing of the water, so I'm not sure it's comparable.

I'm trying to think of a way to test this hypothesis, but what I will note is that even very dry air does not seem to be unable to hold an etheric charge. Nor does air conditioned air seem to lose this ability. When I lived with someone who insisted on using AC I was able to keep my room tolerable by means of incense, but something drains it fairly quickly.

replies.title: Re: AC, air humidity, and etheric energy
replies.content: I wonder, though, if it's specifically a function of running the air through metal coils. Metal conducts etheric energy and may well strip some of it from the air.

replies.title: Re: AC, air humidity, and etheric energy
replies.content:
This has a fairly simple test: it's moderately common to use heat pumps to heat buildings, and heat pumps set to warm air are functionally air conditioners in reverse; they run the air past the same set of metal coils as an air conditioner. If the issue is running air through metal coils, they should also devitalize the air.

It would be interesting to see if anyone writing here has heat pump based heating, and if they've noticed any effect on the energetic quality of the air.

replies.title: Re: AC, air humidity, and etheric energy
replies.content: That's a good point. I'm not in a position to be any help, though, as I've lived for all my adult life in buildings heated by steam radiators.

replies.title: Re: AC, air humidity, and etheric energy
replies.content:
If it helps, Wilhelm Reich found, when doing his many experiments that later formed his theory of orgone, that humidity was a critical component of whether the experiments worked or not. In a naturally dry environment, such as high latitudes, or high altitude, the orgone or etheric technology worked, in a humid environment, they don't.

It is probably because the etheric life force condenses out before being useful. So an AC unit is producing dead air.

replies.title: Re: AC, air humidity, and etheric energy
replies.content:
I wonder if that links up with the observation that Jerusalem was the place where the temple priesthood leaned really hard on trying to extirpate the influence of all foreign gods and all not-Temple-distributed yeast and all mildew, rather than this having been historically observed of a centralized priesthood in some rainforest somewhere.

Come to think of it, wasn't there some other tradition where menstruating women were kept at some remove from ritual practices that was also in an arid or semi-arid place?

Coming in last few minutes. I’m only 30, but I’ve become aware of astrology’s power in shaping our lives and my ability to work with the stars in my life. E.G., a Jupiter trining my natal Venus in the 6th house can promise a great work venture if I time an interview within the transit.

How should I use this knowledge? I expect making plans for how to spend the next 30 years before my next Saturn return is trying to plan too much over a period guaranteed to gift me with unexpected events and encounters, but I’m curious if I should keep that info in mind.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I recommend, first, getting a thorough delineation from a competent natal astrologer, and second, getting an annual progressed chart. Between them, these will allow you to use the strengths in your horoscope to your best advantage while shoring up the weak spots.

Is there an existing alchemical term for a similarity-based separation procedure, where you provide an example principle (possibly two example principles, a positive one and a negative one), and then the procedure turns that example into some distinction in some way you don't necessarily have access to the details of, and then the procedure uses that distinction to separate a substance into one part that is more like the example and one part that is less?

Like in immunoseparation/immunofractionation, where you use a vertebrate's immune system to define the distinction from the example by inventing antibodies and maybe screening them to prevent false-positive antibodies, and then you retain whatever sticks to the antibodies you picked. Or taking a slightly supersaturated solution with one component that you wanted to crystallize and another component you didn't, such that both things would formed crystals if they had somewhere to start, and mixing in tons of ground-up seed crystals for one substance and not the other so that you could then separate the substances with a filter that passes liquids and blocks solids, even if you didn't know what either substance quite was.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, I'm not familiar with any such process in alchemy.

comments.title: Paedrig's shrine to Jupiter
comments.content:
The ritual to consecrate the shrine to Jupiter was completed last Thorsday. By Jove, I'd say you were right about him making an appearance!

I did the ritual exactly as you instructed, in the way of the ancient Greeks. I used sage as the incense, as one of the sweet herbs in the wreath I placed upon the obelisk in the midst of the shrine before anointing it with oil.

As I began the ritual, when the hour of jupiter struck, the clouds began to darken. As I poured forth the oil and began the orphic hymn, it began to rain. When I poured the libation, lightning and thunder began to roll across the sky.

This was real and true, I swear to you. I didnt exactly hear the voice of Jupiter thunder out of the sky, but after thanking him for his presence, consecrating the shrine to him, and asking for his blessing, I swear I heard the echo of the thinder as voice in my mind, advising me to stand tall, that I was worthy to stand in the presence of the gods.

It was a profound experience, and I thank you for guiding me to it.

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's shrine to Jupiter
replies.content:
Delighted to hear it! It interests me (and delights me) that the Greek and Roman gods seem to be resurgent these days.

And I've always liked the fact that the ancients prayed on their feet, rather than groveling on their knees.
